parent.whyItMatters: Recalling small sums and differences without counting frees up your child's attention for bigger ideas later — place value, adding within 20, and word problems all lean on this being automatic. Fluency here is the difference between 'can work it out' and 'just knows it'.
parent.howToTeach: Start concrete (fingers, blocks, buttons), move to spoken facts, then to written ones. Keep sessions short — five minutes of quick-fire facts beats twenty minutes of worksheets. Celebrate speed AND accuracy, but never trade one for the other.
watchFor: Recounting from 1 every time instead of 'counting on' from the larger number — a sign the facts aren't yet memorised.
Always using fingers even for facts like 2+1 — fine early on, but the goal is recall.
Reversing subtraction (answering 5 for '2 − 5') — check they understand you can't take away more than you have, at this level.
tryAtHome: Snack maths: 'You have 3 grapes, I give you 2 more — how many now?'
Number-bond flashes: hold up fingers, ask 'how many more to make 5?'
Ten-second challenge: how many within-5 facts can they answer in ten seconds?

student.intro: When we ADD, we put groups together to get more. When we SUBTRACT, we take some away to get less. Let's get so good at the small ones — all the way up to 5 — that you just KNOW them, quick as a blink!

examples.say: 2 apples and 3 more apples. Push them together… 5 apples! So 2 + 3 = 5.
5 fish, 2 swim away, 3 are left. So 5 − 2 = 3.
3 wants to become 5. How many more? 2! Because 3 and 2 make 5.

masteryCriteria: Answer 2 + 3 quickly without counting on fingers
Answer 5 – 2 from recall or with minimal counting
Complete a set of within-5 addition/subtraction facts accurately and quickly

items.prompt: Quick-fire: answer each within-5 fact. (auto-generated set)
You have 4 stickers and give 1 away. How many are left?
